title: Prompt Engineering Capstone
concept: Combining format control, few-shot examples, and CoT into one production-grade prompt; 'Next up: Advanced Prompting.'.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson on sampling parameters, what happens when you lower the temperature parameter closer to a temperature of 0.0?

options.text: It makes the output much more creative and random
It makes the output more deterministic and consistent
It increases the maximum number of tokens the model can generate

exercises.explain: Lowering the temperature reduces randomness, making the model's output more focused, consistent, and predictable.

cards.title: The Grand Finale
cards.body: We've learned about formatting, few-shot examples, and temperature settings. Now, it's time to combine all these individual ingredients into one ultimate, production-ready master prompt!

cards.title: The Capstone Prompt
cards.body: A prompt capstone brings together formatting rules, few-shot examples, and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. This combined approach guides the model's structure, accuracy, and step-by-step logic all at once.

cards.title: The Master Recipe Analogy
cards.body: Think of a master prompt like a recipe in a busy restaurant. You specify the format (plated on a blue dish), provide examples of what it should look like (a photo of a cooked meal), and write down the step-by-step cooking steps.

cards.title: Putting It All Together
cards.body: We instruct the AI to parse invoice emails. We write: '1. Think step-by-step to extract numbers. 2. Output only valid JSON. 3. Here is an example: Email -> {"total": 50}. Now analyze this new email.'

cards.title: More is Not Always Better
cards.body: You might think adding more instructions always makes the model perform better. In reality, prompts can get too cluttered; keep your combined instructions clean, structured, and free of contradictions.

cards.title: Basic vs. Master Prompt
cards.body: Compare a basic, single-instruction prompt with a professional, structured master prompt.

left.text: 'Explain photosynthesis and output JSON.' (Prone to formatting errors and poor explanation steps)

right.text: 'Think step-by-step. Use <thinking> tags. Output JSON inside <json> tags. Here is an example...'

exercises.prompt: Why do production-grade prompts often use XML-like tags (such as <examples> or <instructions>)?

options.text: They make the prompt look colorful in the code editor
They separate instructions, examples, and input data so the model doesn't get confused
They automatically lower the API cost of the prompt
They replace the need for chain-of-thought reasoning entirely

exercises.explain: Structured tags like XML help the model easily distinguish where instructions, examples, and user data start and end, preventing confusion.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Including few-shot examples helps the model understand the exact format output you want.
exercises.explain: Yes, few-shot examples show the model the desired formatting in action, which reinforces the written format instructions.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing piece of a combined prompt strategy:
exercises.sentence: To get reliable structured outputs for complex tasks, we combine chain-of-thought reasoning with strict format control.
exercises.explain: When we want the model to solve complex problems and then output a specific format, we combine chain-of-thought reasoning with format control instructions.

exercises.prompt: Which of these prompts represents a complete, production-grade combined prompt pattern?

options.text: Translate this text to Spanish: 'Hello, how are you today?'

You are an expert classifier. First, think step-by-step inside <thinking> tags. Then, output the final label inside <output> tags. Example: Input: 'Love this!' -> <thinking>Positive tone</thinking><output>POS</output>. Here is the new input: 'I hate waiting.'
Write a short poem about a prompt engineer who is very tired of writing system instructions. Use a funny tone.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a complete, production-ready system prompt that combines chain-of-thought instructions, a few-shot example, and strict output formatting. Option A is a basic request, and Option C is just a simple instruction without examples or CoT.

exercises.prompt: You are building a production-grade customer support assistant that must output clean, structured JSON data. How do you design your prompt for maximum reliability?

options.text: By only using few-shot examples and ignoring formatting instructions entirely
By turning the temperature to 0.0 and letting the model guess the format
By combining XML format tags, concrete examples, and chain-of-thought instructions together
By writing a very short, one-sentence prompt with no context

exercises.explain: A production-grade prompt brings format control, examples, and step-by-step thinking instructions together. If we want structured JSON output, we must explicitly request JSON format control in the prompt, or the LLM might return plain conversational text instead. Next up: Advanced Prompting.
